nav_mobile_description: Explore datasets, projects, and community work from the Commons.
hero_title_prefix: A Digital Commons for the Hong Kong
hero_description: Empowering communities to build with Hong Kong's richest open geospatial datasets. Human-centered, archival quality, and community-owned.
hero_visual_alt: Architectural drawing of a Hong Kong tower with measured annotations
foundation_title: Foundation for a Smarter, Accessible City
foundation_description: We believe geospatial infrastructure should be a public utility, designed with an editorial eye for our resplendent city.
foundation_public_data_description: Accessible, enriched, and ready to use—we stand on the shoulder of giants, so you can stand on ours.
foundation_community_apps_description: Community projects that bridge data and lived experience: maps, stories, tools, and everything in between.
foundation_humane_tech_description:
Big Tech built the wrong things.
Here's how we're building instead.
showcase_description: From Korean BBQ to public spaces—a searchable, location-aware directory of places across the city.
showcase_cultural_mapping_description: Over 400 neon signs documented and mapped. More are added every day.
showcase_transit_flow_description: A spatial dataset of over 4,000 street names and the stories behind them.
newsletter_description: Get notified about new datasets, meetups, and community-led mapping projects.
newsletter_success_body: Check your inbox for the confirmation email.
newsletter_privacy: We use Substack. Read their privacy policy for details.
footer_description: A grassroots initiative dedicated to the documentation and visualization of Hong Kong's urban fabric through open geospatial standards.
manifesto_subtitle: Thirteen principles to honour the social fabric, memory, and future of our city.
manifesto_1_body:
A dense, ravishing city with deep cultural roots and a pressing need to reinvent itself. Its people have done so before, time and again—turning constraint into character, density into diversity, harbour into home.
SaanSeoi is our contribution to that future.
manifesto_2_body: Hong Kong changes fast enough to forget itself. The record of what was is inseparable from any account of what is—patterns of change, loss, and renewal only become legible across time. Official datasets have blind spots; community knowledge fills them. We hold both with care, treating the past as essential infrastructure.
manifesto_3_body: Every dataset encodes choices—what to measure, what to name, who to count. Geospatial data is especially loaded: it fixes boundaries, asserts presence, and renders others invisible. We build with that awareness, and stay honest about the trade-offs.
manifesto_4_lead: Social fabric is the core weave
manifesto_4_body:
A map of Hong Kong without its people is just geometry. The social fabric that makes this city pulse exists because of the bonds, trust, and restless energy accumulated over generations. Technology that erodes that fabric in the name of efficiency is a regression.
We build to strengthen the weave.
manifesto_5_body: The tools we adopt shape our attention, our relationships, our sense of place. We choose systems that amplify <u>curiosity</u>, <u>care</u>, and <u>collective capacity</u>—a trinity to strengthen the weave. But no local effort is insulated from the systems it sits within.
manifesto_6_body: The open web was captured by a handful of platforms. Our societies are still reckoning with the harm this concentration caused. This state is not final. We use the digital commons as the staging site for our intervention: to reseed agency with the people the internet was meant for.
manifesto_7_body: Every advancement builds on what came before. The digital commons is the save state of that effort in the digital realm—a shared inheritance, free for all to build upon. But inheritance only has value if it can be accessed.
manifesto_8_body: When data is locked behind expertise, paywalls, or needless complexity, that virtuous cycle of advancement is broken. What is gained in private value is lost in civic, social, and cultural capital. The barrier to entry should be as close to zero as possible.
manifesto_9_body: Open standards and shared foundations let efforts compound. When systems talk to each other, everyone builds forward instead of starting over.
manifesto_10_body: A startup routing deliveries, a journalist mapping neighborhood change, an artist visualizing air quality—these users validate the commons more than any scale metric. The city is best understood at the scale it is lived in.
manifesto_11_body: The model where open data producers and consumers are strangers needs a reset. We treat data work as care work. The invisible parts (speccing, compilation, validation) are now informed by visible ones (outreach, facilitation, tending). This bridges the last mile from data to application, and keeps effort focused where it matters.
manifesto_12_body: Everything we create encodes a choice—what to prioritise, what to preserve, who to serve. We make those choices with the community we build for, and answer to it when we get them wrong. There is no neutral position. The only question is whether the choices are made with care, in public, and in service of the city.
manifesto_13_body:
The next few years will determine whether global developments enrich or erase the city we live in. That outcome isn't settled by policy or platforms alone—it's steered by what gets built at street level, by people who know what's at stake.
So we show up, and we build.
